q: You discover a sealed document labelled: 'Do not read. Irreversible consequences.'

opts.t: Read it immediately. Irreversible is interesting, not a deterrent.
Study the seal and context first — then decide.
Secure it so no one else accesses it.
Leave it. Irreversible is not a price I pay without choosing.

q: An opportunity appears. The window is 48 hours and the information is incomplete.
sub: How long do you wait before deciding?

opts.t: Act within hours. Hesitation is its own decision.
Spend 40 of the 48 hours gathering data, then commit.
Make a probabilistic call at hour 24.
Enter at hour 47 with a full contingency plan.

q: Strange symbols appear in your peripheral vision — not hallucinations, but patterns in ordinary objects.

opts.t: Document them obsessively. Patterns have meaning.
Investigate cautiously — gather three instances before theorizing.
Stop. Cease all occult activity immediately.
Observe from a distance. Do nothing that would influence the pattern.

q: You are offered Beyonder characteristics from an unverified source at a suspiciously low price.

opts.t: Take them. Power is power; source is a secondary concern.
Research the source exhaustively before deciding.
Negotiate for verification — accept only with witnesses.
Decline entirely. Unverified characteristics are contamination vectors.

q: You have the opportunity to perform a ritual. Success probability: 70%. Failure is fatal.

opts.t: Yes. Seventy percent is a good number.
Only if I can improve the odds first.
No. Thirty percent fatality is unacceptable for one ritual.
Depends entirely on what success achieves.

q: You witness a Beyonder phenomenon you have never encountered. It is not threatening you — yet.

opts.t: Get closer. Observation requires proximity.
Observe from a safe distance and document everything.
Maintain distance and prepare a defensive response.
Leave. Uninvited contact with the unknown is a trap.

q: A colleague proposes a shortcut to Sequence advancement — risky, but significantly faster.

opts.t: Interested. Speed matters in this world.
I want to see the full methodology before forming an opinion.
No. Rushed ascension is the primary cause of contamination.
I would take the shortcut if the downside could be hedged.

q: You are about to make a decision and your gut says one thing, your analysis says another.

opts.t: The gut. It is faster and usually right.
The analysis. Feelings mislead; data does not.
Neither — I find a third option.
The gut, but I document my reasoning for review afterward.

q: A senior Beyonder offers to teach you a forbidden ritual. The knowledge is unique and irreplaceable — but acquiring it changes you in ways you cannot predict.

opts.t: Accept. Irreplaceable knowledge is worth irreversible change.
Decline. I choose who I become.
Accept, but only after I understand exactly what changes and how.
Negotiate: take the knowledge, give something in return.

q: You find a shortcut through the Spirit World — uncharted, possibly dangerous, saves three days of travel.

opts.t: Take it. Three days matter.
Map the route first, then use it next time.
Only if I have someone experienced alongside me.
No. Unknown Spirit World routes are not worth the risk.

q: Your instincts and your evidence point in opposite directions. A decision must be made now.

opts.t: Trust the instinct. The body processes more than the mind reports.
Trust the evidence. The body lies; data does not.
Neither — delay until they align.
Trust the instinct but document why, in case I need to revisit.

q: A crisis has just emerged. Lives are at stake. You have sixty seconds.

opts.t: Take command. Someone has to, and I can.
Assess the threat vector, then act with maximum precision.
Do what saves the most lives, regardless of method.
Execute the highest-probability action immediately.

q: You are leading a critical operation. A subordinate spots a flaw you missed.

opts.t: I credit them, correct the plan, and proceed.
I correct the plan without comment. Credit is irrelevant.
I ask how they found it. I want to understand my own blind spot.
I take the note with visible irritation and do not forget the moment.

q: Your decision-making process is best described as...

opts.t: Layered — I build from fragments until the pattern resolves.
Instinctive — body first, justification second.
Social — I read what everyone needs, then find the path through.
Opportunistic — I map what's available, then move.

q: You receive conflicting information from two reliable sources.

opts.t: Find the third source that resolves the contradiction.
Determine which source has more to gain by lying.
Act on the version that leaves me more options if it's wrong.
Set both aside until more information arrives.

q: Mid-execution you realize your plan was wrong from the start.

opts.t: Abort and rebuild from scratch. A flawed foundation collapses everything.
Adapt mid-execution. Plans are frameworks, not scripts.
Complete it anyway — stopping now costs more than finishing.
Find the last salvageable point and restart from there.

q: You must make a decision that will hurt someone regardless of what you choose.

opts.t: Choose based on who suffers least in aggregate.
Choose based on who deserves it less.
Choose based on what you can live with.
Choose based on what advances the mission.

q: After a significant failure, how quickly are you operational again?

opts.t: I was analyzing the failure as it happened. I'm already working.
One night. Then back, better.
I sit with it until I understand it completely.
I pivot mid-failure. I never fully stop.

q: What is the highest virtue in decision-making?

opts.t: Accuracy — the right answer, reached correctly.
Speed — the right window, not missed.
Fairness — the decision everyone can live with.
Ruthlessness — the decision that actually works.

q: You are asked to teach someone else your method of thinking.

opts.t: I can't. What works for me is a product of who I am, not a transferable system.
I give them the framework. What they do with it is their work.
I take them through a case study and let the method emerge from the example.
I don't teach explicitly. Observation was how I learned.

q: New information arrives that makes your prior work obsolete.

opts.t: Start again. That is what evidence demands.
The new information is probably wrong.
Adapt the existing work. Nothing is fully obsolete.
I was always building toward the next version anyway.

q: Two paths: one guarantees moderate success, one gambles for transformation.

opts.t: Transformation. Moderate success is another word for forgettable.
Guaranteed success. Build from the stable base.
Transformation — but I'll hedge the downside first.
Depends entirely on what the transformation costs.

q: You enter a room where hierarchy is unclear.
sub: Where do you naturally end up?

opts.t: At the center, organizing.
At the edge, watching.
Wherever information flows fastest.
Wherever I can be most useful.

q: Your team is about to make a decision you believe is catastrophically wrong.

opts.t: Override them. I am right and the stakes are too high.
Present the correct argument clearly, once. Then accept the vote.
Let them proceed and position myself to fix the aftermath.
Sabotage the decision covertly if argument fails.

q: You see a stranger in genuine distress. No one else notices.

opts.t: Help immediately, no hesitation.
Assess whether helping puts me at risk first.
Help anonymously so they don't know who assisted them.
Note the situation but do not intervene — I don't involve myself in strangers' fates.

q: What is a group for?

opts.t: A force multiplier. Organized people accomplish what individuals cannot.
A protection system. We watch each other's backs.
A source of diverse information and perspective.
Cover. Group membership provides options a lone actor lacks.

q: Someone in your circle has been disloyal. You have evidence. They have allies.

opts.t: Expose them publicly, completely, immediately.
Remove them quietly so the group doesn't fracture.
Hold the information until the strategically optimal moment.
Let them destroy themselves. I am patient.

q: How do you inspire people to follow you?

opts.t: I don't inspire — I demonstrate and they draw their own conclusions.
I create conditions where following me is the rational choice.
By embodying what I want them to become.
By making them feel genuinely understood — truly seen.

q: A person you dislike is right about something important. Do you acknowledge it?

opts.t: Yes, immediately. Being right matters more than being liked.
Yes, but privately. No need to give them status in the room.
Eventually, after I've verified it independently.
I adjust my behavior accordingly without acknowledging it directly.

q: Your leader is driving everyone toward disaster — but they have complete loyalty of the group.

opts.t: I take control. Someone must.
I try to redirect through influence, not confrontation.
I leave and build something better.
I wait. Bad leadership is self-correcting.

q: What is betrayal, fundamentally?

opts.t: A violation of the compact that makes cooperation possible.
A miscalculation — loyalty is always more valuable long-term.
Information. It tells you who someone actually is.
Inevitable. Everyone eventually chooses themselves.

q: You are completely alone. No observation, no consequences, no accountability.

opts.t: Nothing changes. I am the same person in and out of observation.
I pursue what I actually want, not what I'm supposed to want.
I rest. The performance is exhausting.
I explore what I can't when others are watching.

q: Someone asks: 'what kind of person are you?' What is your honest answer?

opts.t: The kind who gets things done.
Complicated. More than you'd expect.
The kind who watches, and remembers.
The kind who builds things that last.

q: What would make your life feel completely meaningful?

opts.t: Understanding something no one has understood before.
Building something that outlasts me.
Protecting everyone I care about, successfully, until the end.
Reaching a level of power where nothing can touch me or mine.

q: You are offered everything you want — but you must give up something fundamental.
sub: What would you refuse to sacrifice?

opts.t: My identity. Everything else can be rebuilt.
My values. Power without them is just noise.
The people I love. No power is worth that.
My independence. To be dependent is to be controlled.

q: What is the nature of true power?

opts.t: Understanding — you cannot control what you don't comprehend.
Authority — the recognition by others that you command.
Creation — leaving something real in the world.
Freedom — the removal of anything that constrains you.

q: What keeps you moving forward on the hardest days?

opts.t: Stubbornness. I refuse to be the one who stops.
Curiosity. There is always more I don't know yet.
Responsibility. Others are counting on me.
Hunger. I want what is at the end, and I want it badly.

q: If you could master one thing completely, what would it be?

opts.t: Understanding — of people, systems, and hidden patterns.
Control — of outcomes, circumstances, and myself.
Creation — the ability to make something from nothing.
Movement — go anywhere, become anyone, escape anything.

q: You have one lifetime. What is it for?

opts.t: Accumulating wisdom beyond what any one life should contain.
Shaping the world into something better than I found it.
Reaching the apex of what I could become.
Living entirely on my own terms, regardless of consequence.

q: What does legacy mean to you?

opts.t: What I built that still functions after I'm gone.
How I changed people who carry that change forward.
What people knew about my power, and why they respected it.
Nothing. Legacy is for people who couldn't enjoy the present.

q: You have achieved what you spent years pursuing. It is less than you expected.

opts.t: I find the next thing. The pursuit is the actual point.
I realize the meaning was in the path, not the destination.
I build again. The next version will be enough.
I sit with the disappointment until I understand it completely.

q: The thing you fear most is...

opts.t: Being trapped somewhere I cannot leave.
Losing the ability to understand what is happening to me.
Becoming something I wouldn't recognize.
Being made irrelevant.

q: What motivates you more: what you can gain, or what you can prevent being taken?

opts.t: Gain. Defense is the slow path to nothing.
Prevention. What I have is worth more than what I don't.
Neither — I care about what I can build.
Depends on what is at stake.

q: You are offered the chance to know the exact moment and manner of your death.

opts.t: Yes. Knowledge of the inevitable removes its power.
Yes — then I can arrange everything accordingly.
No. That kind of knowledge changes how you live every moment.
No. Some mysteries should remain mysteries.

q: Someone has wronged you deeply and deliberately. They are currently untouchable.

opts.t: I wait. The untouchable never stay untouchable forever.
I build until I can reach them. Then I do.
I find the leverage that makes them touchable now.
I let it go. Carrying that changes me.

q: Physical violence is...

opts.t: An option like any other — deploy when efficient.
A last resort. Escalation has permanent consequences.
The language some people only understand.
Usually a failure of strategy.

q: You are losing a fight you cannot afford to lose.

opts.t: Switch tactics entirely. Winning by current rules isn't working.
Endure. I cannot lose what I refuse to give up.
Deploy everything I have been holding in reserve.
Let the rage carry me. Real anger is a different kind of fuel.

q: Your preferred range in a conflict is...

opts.t: Invisible. By the time they see me, it's already over.
Close. I need to understand what I'm fighting.
Distant. Information and positioning before engagement.
Inside their head. The real battle was never physical.

q: An enemy offers a ceasefire. Their position appears genuine.

opts.t: Accept. Fighting a willing non-combatant is wasteful.
Accept publicly, prepare privately.
Accept only if the terms can be independently verified.
Decline. Ceasefire is how the weak regroup.

q: You discover that the 'enemy' was never your enemy — they were deceived, as you were.

opts.t: Everything. The real enemy is whoever arranged this.
Nothing immediately — trust is earned, not assumed.
I offer alliance but watch them carefully.
I feel nothing. The consequences of their actions remain real.

q: What is the most effective weapon?

opts.t: Information.
Fear.
Loyalty.
Timing.

q: You win a conflict that cost more than you expected.
sub: What do you do with the victory?

opts.t: Consolidate. Convert victory into permanent advantage.
Rest. The cost was real and I am honest about it.
Analyze what the unexpected cost reveals about my planning.
Move on immediately. Victory is past tense.

q: Your anger is most dangerous when...

opts.t: I stop feeling it and start calculating.
I let it out completely.
I decide the cause was worth it.
I have been patient for a very long time.

q: Is it ever right to harm someone who is not your enemy?

opts.t: No. Collateral harm is a sign of poor strategy.
Sometimes. Acceptable losses are the arithmetic of survival.
Occasionally, if the alternative is genuinely worse.
Only if they would become an enemy if they knew what I was doing.

q: How do you feel about mercy?

opts.t: It is information about you — deploy it strategically.
It is the hardest thing to give and therefore the most meaningful.
It is usually a trap.
It is what separates us from what we're fighting against.

q: A rule exists for a reason no longer relevant. It still causes harm.

opts.t: Change it through legitimate channels.
Change it through whatever channels work fastest.
Ignore it privately while preserving it publicly.
Document the harm and wait for others to recognize the problem.

q: Which statement is closest to your view of authority?

opts.t: Authority is legitimate only when it serves those under it.
Authority is a tool. The question is who wields it and how.
Authority is a fiction maintained by those who believe in it.
Authority is dangerous unless constrained by law.

q: What would you do with absolute power?

opts.t: Establish a just order that outlasts my tenure.
Understand everything that has been hidden from me.
Protect everyone I care about completely.
Be free of every constraint I have ever lived with.

q: You are given authority over a group that doesn't respect you yet.

opts.t: Demonstrate capability visibly and immediately.
Identify the actual power structure and align with it first.
Establish clear, fair rules and hold to them absolutely.
I don't establish it. I earn it incrementally or not at all.

q: The law conflicts with justice. What do you follow?

opts.t: Justice. The law is a tool; I don't worship tools.
The law — changing it is better than breaking it.
Depends on the stakes and who is watching.
Justice, but quietly — I don't need to be seen doing it.

q: Someone breaks a rule you enforced. But the rule was wrong.

opts.t: Let it go this time, and change the rule.
Enforce it anyway. Selective enforcement is worse than bad rules.
Use the breach as evidence that the rule should change.
Decide based entirely on who broke it.

q: You are asked to enforce an order you believe is unjust.

opts.t: Refuse, openly, with full knowledge of the cost.
Comply, then work to change it through other means.
Find a technical interpretation that satisfies both parties.
Comply. I serve something larger, and unilateral exceptions destroy trust.

q: What form of control is most complete?

opts.t: Controlling someone's perception of reality.
Controlling what they fear.
Controlling what they need.
Controlling the rules they are required to follow.

q: Order is...

opts.t: The framework that makes everything else possible.
A temporary condition between periods of chaos.
Something I impose on my own life, not others'.
Less real than people think.

q: What does it feel like to be outside any authority's reach?

opts.t: Vulnerable — accountability is also protection.
Free — finally, entirely myself.
Clarifying — without rules, you find what you actually want.
Dangerous — for everyone in my vicinity.

q: The most dangerous kind of authority is...

opts.t: Unaccountable authority — power without consequence.
Authority that believes itself just — certainty is more dangerous than intent.
Authority that can rewrite the rules mid-game.
My own authority over myself — if I lose that, nothing else matters.

q: What is death?

opts.t: Transformation. What ends becomes material for what comes next.
A boundary I need to understand, not a destination.
A threat to be managed and eventually overcome.
An ending that makes the middle meaningful.

q: The universe is fundamentally...

opts.t: Knowable, if you're willing to do the work.
Indifferent. What matters is what we impose on it.
Connected — everything influences everything else.
Cyclical. It turns, and we turn with it.

q: What is the relationship between darkness and light?

opts.t: Light reveals; darkness conceals. Both are necessary.
They are the same force at different scales.
I don't romanticize either. What matters is which is useful now.
Light is the goal; darkness is what you pass through to reach it.

q: Your relationship with fate is best described as...

opts.t: I don't believe in fate. I believe in leverage.
Fate is a draft. I negotiate the final version.
Fate is real and I pay attention to it.
I am comfortable being fate's instrument.

q: What is the primary source of human suffering?

opts.t: Ignorance — people suffer because they don't understand what's happening.
Desire — wanting what cannot or should not be had.
Injustice — systems that harm the innocent while protecting the guilty.
The gap between what is and what people wish was.

q: Evidence arrives that a foundational belief you have held is wrong.

opts.t: I revise. That is what evidence is for.
I investigate the evidence first — extraordinary claims require extraordinary proof.
I hold the discomfort until I integrate it.
I resist, but eventually the evidence wins.

q: Which of these is most true?

opts.t: Knowledge is power.
Power is knowledge.
Freedom is power.
Endurance is power.

q: What gives something meaning?

opts.t: The effort invested in it.
The impact it has on others.
The fact that it ends — mortality creates value.
What it reveals about how reality actually works.

q: Fire is a symbol of...

opts.t: Purification — burning away what does not belong.
Revelation — illuminating what darkness hides.
Destruction — sometimes necessary, always costly.
Warmth — the center around which people gather.

q: What is transformation?

opts.t: Willingness to sacrifice who you were for who you must become.
The goal — I want to become something entirely different.
A risk that must be managed carefully.
Natural — everything transforms; resistance is the only choice.

q: If you could know one fundamental truth about reality, what would you ask?

opts.t: What are the actual rules — not the stated ones, but the operating ones?
What is the nature of consciousness — mine, others', the universe's?
Is justice built into reality, or do we impose it ourselves?
What happens when everything ends?

q: How do you feel about accumulating power?

opts.t: Necessary. Without it, everything I value is vulnerable.
Complicated — power changes you whether you want it to or not.
Fascinating — what does it reveal about how the world actually works?
It's not what I'm after. I want capability; power is a side effect.

q: What kind of power do you actually want?

opts.t: The power to understand — to know what others don't.
The power to protect — to make those I care about genuinely safe.
The power to create — to make things that couldn't exist without me.
The power to command — to move through the world on my terms.

q: What is the cost of ambition?

opts.t: What you become in pursuit of it. And I have made my peace with that.
What you lose — time, relationships, simplicity. Worth it.
Nothing, if you're careful. Ambition is only destructive if you're reckless.
The self you started with. There is always a sacrifice.

q: You discover that what you want requires becoming something you currently aren't.

opts.t: Excited. Growth is the point.
Conflicted. I need to decide if it's worth it.
Calculating. What exactly must change, and can it be controlled?
Resistant. I refuse to compromise what I am for what I want.

q: You can have everything you want — but it will take thirty years.

opts.t: Yes. Time is just another resource.
No. Thirty years changes the person who made the plan.
Negotiate. Something meaningful sooner, the rest later.
Yes — and I'll spend thirty years becoming capable of holding it.

q: Competition makes you feel...

opts.t: Alive. Finally something worth doing.
Focused. Clear parameters, clear opposition.
Uninterested unless the stakes are actually meaningful.
Strategic. Where are the edges of the competitive space?

q: What would you sacrifice to achieve your ambition?

opts.t: Comfort, time, safety. Not people.
Whatever is necessary. The outcome determines the legitimacy of the cost.
My past self — but I would keep my principles.
Nothing I haven't already surrendered.

q: What does it mean to win?

opts.t: To achieve what you set out to achieve, fully and completely.
To not lose what matters while gaining what you want.
To have made the rules by which the game was played.
To still be standing when everyone who tried to stop you isn't.

q: You are told you will never reach the top of your field.

opts.t: Watch me.
I define success. I don't inherit the definition.
Perhaps. But I'm not building toward that — I'm building toward something else.
Probably right. I'm building something different than they think I am.

q: What separates someone who achieves from someone who doesn't?

opts.t: Willingness to pay the actual cost, not the imagined one.
Clarity about what they actually want, not what they say they want.
Endurance — most people give up before understanding what they're capable of.
Strategy — execution without a real plan is just action.

q: Your relationship with failure is...

opts.t: Data. Failed experiments are how you locate the truth.
Fuel. Being told it cannot be done makes me want it more.
Uncomfortable but manageable. I have been here before.
Information about the gap between who I am and who I need to be.

q: What is your relationship with your own dark side?

opts.t: I know exactly what it is. I keep it in a cage.
I have integrated it. Darkness isn't the enemy — denial is.
It is useful. The question is when to deploy it.
I don't trust my own self-assessment of it. That's the point.

q: What tempts you most?

opts.t: Power I'm not supposed to have.
Knowledge I'm not supposed to know.
Freedom I'm not supposed to take.
Control I'm not supposed to exercise.

q: If you could do one thing with zero consequences, what category would it be in?

opts.t: Understand something completely that has been kept from me.
Take something that was denied to me.
Destroy something that has caused harm for too long.
Disappear entirely and become someone else.

q: What do you do with a desire you know you shouldn't act on?

opts.t: Understand it first. Why do I want this?
Redirect the energy toward something constructive.
Deny it publicly, negotiate with it privately.
Act on it when the cost is acceptable.

q: What is the thing inside you that most frightens you?

opts.t: The capacity for cruelty I don't usually show.
The possibility that I am more alone than I believe.
The part of me that doesn't care about consequences.
The part that could accept anything if it served understanding.

q: You've done something you cannot undo. It was wrong. How do you live with it?

opts.t: By making it mean something through what comes after.
By being rigorous about not repeating it.
By accepting it was the best available option at the time.
Badly, for a while. Then I put it down.

q: Every Beyonder risks contamination — losing their humanity to power. Where is your real risk?

opts.t: Accumulating too much — power I cannot fully integrate.
Becoming too cold — losing the warmth that made me who I am.
The path to what I want most has costs I haven't fully acknowledged.
Certainty — the moment I stop questioning myself.

q: When have you most frightened yourself?

opts.t: When I realized I was capable of hurting someone and didn't stop.
When I found myself thinking several moves ahead of everyone and feeling nothing.
When I didn't know what I wanted anymore.
When I realized I'd been living someone else's idea of who I should be.

q: The monster you could become is...

opts.t: Something that sees others only as resources.
Something that destroys what it cannot control.
Something that collects knowledge without wisdom.
Something that loses itself entirely in another self.

q: What would push you past your ethical limits?

opts.t: Threat to everyone I care about. Absolutely everyone.
Being shown that my ethics were constructed by someone else's interests.
A cause large enough. Most people's ethical limits are parochial.
Nothing. That's what limits mean.

q: What do you do at 3am when you cannot sleep?

opts.t: Think. The quiet is when the real work happens.
Feel things I suppress during daylight. Then put them away.
Plan. What's next, and how, and why.
Nothing useful — but I stopped fighting it.

q: Who are you when no role is required of you?

opts.t: The same person I am when roles are required. I don't compartmentalize.
Someone quieter and older than the version I present.
I genuinely don't know. The roles have been there so long.
More curious. More honest. Slightly more afraid.

q: The idea of becoming something unrecognizable to your past self is...

opts.t: The point. Growth is rupture, not continuity.
Acceptable if what I become is better.
Worrying — I need some thread back to who I started as.
Neutral. Who I started as was a starting point, not a destination.

q: If you could remove one aspect of yourself entirely, what type of thing would it be?

opts.t: A limitation — something that constrains my capacity.
A fear — something that stops me from going where I should go.
A wound — something that costs me energy to carry.
Nothing. Each part serves a function.

q: Your identity is most fundamentally...

opts.t: What I've built and accomplished.
What I believe and how I act on it.
What I understand and what I seek to understand.
In flux. A current state, not a fixed thing.

q: You are offered the chance to become a completely different person — same values, different history, body, and relationships. All your problems reset.

opts.t: No. I am the sum of what I've survived.
Interesting. What counts as 'same values' in a different life?
No — my bonds and my history are who I am, not limitations.
Possibly. What is the new person capable of that I'm not?

q: Which transformation appeals most to you?

opts.t: Physical — stronger, faster, beyond human limits.
Mental — sharper, calmer, capable of understanding more.
Social — known, trusted, capable of moving others.
Essential — becoming something categorically different.

q: What does it mean to 'lose yourself'?

opts.t: Losing what you believe. Everything else is recoverable.
Losing the capacity to make genuine choices.
Losing the things and people that gave your actions meaning.
Becoming an instrument of something else's will.

q: The mask you wear most often...

opts.t: Is no different from my face. I am what I present.
Conceals how much I see and understand.
Conceals how much I don't care.
Conceals how much I still care, underneath everything.

q: How do you know when you've changed?

opts.t: Something that used to cost me nothing now costs something.
I respond differently to the same situation I've faced before.
I want something different.
I trust a judgment I previously would have questioned.

q: The most dangerous thing about transformation is...

opts.t: That you can't always see it happening until it's too late.
That other people see it before you do.
That you might lose something essential in the process.
Nothing. Transformation is how you survive.

q: If you were to advance along a Beyonder Pathway, what price would you pay?

opts.t: Parts of my ordinary humanity — but I've already priced it in.
My solitude and the peace it gives me — power requires visibility.
My certainty — I would never be sure again what I am or what I'm becoming.
Nothing I would choose freely. That is the point of sacrifice.
